speaker: interviewer
text: --back for a minute to Hungary, you have applied for the scholarship.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did it come about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it came about, really, in two stages. One stage I saw, the other stage I didn't. And then there is a-- sort of a footnote to it all. I was called in to the US legation. We didn't yet have an embassy. It was called a legation. And I met a very nice gentleman who gave me a written and oral examination in English, and I did very well. And then I didn't hear for a long time and I, frankly, had forgotten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Long time meaning--

speaker: interviewee
text: Long time meaning several months. And then suddenly I got a letter saying that I have been granted a B'nai B'rith Hillel Foundation scholarship that will give me a second class train ticket from Budapest to Cherbourg, France. It will give me a bunk bed on a converted troop ship, the from Cherbourg to New York, that I will be met there by a family who will put me up for the night, and then I will get a train ticket from New York to what I now know is Seattle. I pronounced it 'See-tl' at the time. I got a train ticket from New York to 'See-tl' on the Pacemaker and the Empire Builder. I'll never forget it. And when I get to 'See-tl,' I will have to work in a Jewish fraternity house for my room and board, but they will pay for my tuition, and I will get-- I forget now, a-- a very modest amount of pocket money. And it sounded like a dream. My girlfriend at the time, who is now my wife, came to the train station with me. We both cried. [LAUGHS] Then I got on the train. I never forget it. Vienna, which is so splendid and-- and luxurious, was a rotten, rotten poor, decrepit town. I couldn't buy anything. I had no money at the train station. And I got some very sort of lukewarm water out of the tap, which was my drink. I had some bread and salami that I brought with me from Hungary, and that was my food all the way through. As a matter of fact, when-- when my ship arrived in New York, and this is the only moment when I could have turned profoundly anti-American, they took away had the remaining half of my salami because you're not supposed to bring food in the United States. But I went to Cherbourg and got on the boat. I remember a very large, black sailor was at the gangplank. And I told him my name, and I was on the list, and he said, your bunk is B20. So I went to B20, put down my knapsack. That's all I had. And they called for chow, and I didn't know what chow was, but I sort of figured it out. And at that stage of my life, this was August of '47 when food to me was still the most important thing in this world because I had been deprived of so much food. And we had these huge metal trays on the line, and they gave me all I asked for, and I asked for more. And my tray was just filled with everything-- potatoes and meat and vegetables and bread and milk and pie.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then at the end of the line-- I-- I-- I can see it as clearly as I can see you-- there was a huge wicker basket of oranges and a huge wicker basket of bananas. And my mother taught me to always do the right thing, and I didn't know what the right thing was. There was another huge black sailor, and I said, what's the deal? Can you take an orange, or can you take a banana? Can you make your own choice? And he looked at me and he said, man, you eat all the goddamn bananas and all the goddamn oranges you want. And then I knew I was in paradise.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I arrived in New York, and a very nice lady waited for me, and I went home to her house. The next day, they took me to see the Rockettes at-- at New York City, and then they put me on the train. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Chicago, where I changed trains, I met the head of B'nai B'rith Hilal at the time, who subsequently became president of Brandeis University, Abe Sachar, and his wonderful secretary, Marilyn, who basically picked me on the basis of my papers, so I thanked them. But I have to jump ahead because this is interesting. This was in 1947. I was told by Marilyn that in 1980, that's '57, '67, 33 years later, she had a trip to San Francisco, vacation trip. And she got off the plane and took a cab to the city. And there was a gigantic billboard with my picture and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the suggestion vote for Tom Lantos, Democrat, Congress. And she thought it was a dream.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she called-- she tracked down my campaign headquarters, and she said, is this the Tom Lantos who came? So this is how she found out what happened to the boy she picked in 1947 to get a scholarship. He was running for Congress. And that November, was elected to Congress. Let's jump back a little bit. Did you leave any close friends behind in Hungary?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And also, tell us, how did you meet Annette?

speaker: interviewee
text: I met Annette at a very, very early stage. Our families were friends. I have pictures, as a matter of fact, of the two of us as children in Budapest. We have lots of pictures of the two of us. I don't know how these were saved. Her father, who was born in 1906, and my young uncle, also born in 1906, were schoolmates and friends. And it was through the friendship of Annette's father, who was killed by the Nazis in '44, and my uncle, who was-- who perished on the Russian front, that we got together as very small children. It was obvious to both of us we were going to get married.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who were the friends that you left behind?

speaker: interviewee
text: They fall into several categories. They were friends of my parents or my aunts, and so on. A few distant relatives, one of them still living, the widow of one of my cousins who is in her late 80s now. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These were friends-- not friends in the sense that they were the same age, but they were friends of my parents or friends of-- of the family. And my relationship with practically all of these people was to help them during the course of decades. And I must say that my dear friend in Seattle helped me help them in a very big way. He's a man of considerable means, and when I gave $100, he gave $1,000. But I-- I can never be able to express my appreciation for what he had done for large numbers of people he did not know. He met many of them because he came with me to Hungary after a while, but-- but these were just names, and he wrote checks for them. Then there were my-- my classmates. And in an amazing way, and I can't tell you how and why, at our last class reunion in September of '96, there must have been 20 of my classmates still very much alive and active, with a very interesting footnote. When we had a class reunion several years ago, still under the Communist regime, many of my classmates were in-- were in a retirement status. Now there is a free economy and practically all of them are working again. So it's a very funny thing. I mean, I can think of at least three of them who were physicians, who, when-- when we had the class reunion five years ago, were getting their pension and were retired physicians. Every single one of them is now actively practicing his profession as a physician, which is sort of an interesting social commentary on what a free economy does to people. They want to keep working.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any of these non-Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Yes. At the-- the September meeting they were there. Most of them were Jewish, but some of them were non-Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever discussed with any of these non-Jews what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I didn't because-- because I believe-- well, in the case of one of them who was half-Jewish but somehow were not considered Jewish, his family was a very decent family, and they were horrified and appalled by what was happening. And while I don't think they could do much, they-- they they were on the right side. I cannot tell you of the non-Jews that I had in school as classmates, where they were on the spectrum in terms of bystanders who turned away or bystanders who felt for us but didn't do anything or felt they couldn't do anything. I don't think that any of my Christian friends were active perpetrators of any of the horrors that preoccupied Hungary during that period. These were silent spectators, or maybe in a modest way trying to help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they express ever any sorrow? Or any--

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I suspect once or twice in a-- in a very perfunctory way, but you see, the-- the horror was so overwhelming. These boys like me were kids. They were 16, 17-year-old kids. They must have been horrified. I believe most of them had to be horrified, but they clearly had no power. I don't think if any of their parents had any power. It-- it really didn't become a subject of discussion. And I-- I think-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think it would have been [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very difficult to discuss it, both for them and-- and-- and for us because we could not blame them, but we clearly could not exonerate them or their parents or their grandparents, and-- and while we clearly remembered a great deal. This particular school was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a school with a heavy Jewish enrollment, lots of Christian-Jewish friendships and relationships. And I-- I personally feel that [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] on the-- on-- on the spectrum, these were probably bystanders, mostly, if not all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are they still alive, any of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do they feel about your position today, your power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it's-- it's a very difficult thing to-- to answer because on the one hand, in a political sense, there are two Hungarian-Americans-- well, this is really not accurate because one of them was born here. The-- the current governor of New York, Governor Pataki, is of Hungarian background. He has had no relationship with Hungary and he took his first trip to Hungary, I think, last year, to a village. And I met with him at a-- at a-- at a Yom HaShoah event in New York, and we had a very nice conversation. And I understand that he's a very decent fellow, and he went to his village, and-- and-- but he really has had no Hungarian, really. He doesn't speak Hungarian. I am the other Hungarian-American who politically has a position. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ironically, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there are probably two Hungarian-Americans who are very well known in Hungary. One, because he has done an enormous amount for Hungary through his foundations, and that's my friend George Soros, who is a multi-billionaire and has done unbelievably impressive things for free and open societies beginning in Hungary. I just had lunch with him last week, and we were good friends. He has done an incredible amount for Hungary through his philanthropy, which is very intelligent philanthropy. Now he has a university in Budapest among many other things. And, of course, he is very controversial because no good deed goes unpunished, and he has done many good deeds. When I first went back-- first of all, I've been going back to Hungary regularly since 1964, so I'm really the only American Hungarian who, for the last 32 years, every single year has been back and heavily involved. First, it's just a professor and an interested person, but the last 16 years in a political sense. I have tried to do a great deal for strengthening democracy in Hungary. And while this is not the time or the place because it would take a long time to discuss this, there was a neo-fascist movement in Hungary led by a very talented Hungarian writer, Istvan Csurka, who had a very high position in the leading Hungarian political party several years ago, and I took him on. I had a special session arranged in Congress with a group of my colleagues where we denounced the upsurge of fascism and anti-Semitism. And the Hungarian television carried this with simultaneous translation. And not according to me, but according to many observers in Hungary, I am very happy that I've played a modest role in-- in at least temporarily dealing a setback to neo-fascist anti-Semitic trends in Hungary. I remain very much interested in Hungary. I want Hungary to be a full-fledged member of NATO, a member of the European Union. I want Hungary to be a free and open and democratic society where people of all religions have equal opportunity to achieve anything, so I am very heavily involved with Hungary. I'm very heavily involved with the countries around Hungary. I-- I am, perhaps, the person in Congress who is most interested in this region and who spends the most time on this region, so I've been to all of these areas many, many times and have done a lot of things. But I'm very conscious of the fact that I'm only one of 435 members. And obviously, my-- my power is very limited.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, let's go back to how you get-- how you got to this power. Let's go back to your first arrival to America. You went to Seattle, Washington. And what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, immigrants, I think, fall into two categories. People who never find themselves at home, who always feel that the old country or some idealized version of the old country that never existed is somehow better. Even when they were persecuted, when they were down, and so on, everything is better there and somehow they don't fit. And then there are people-- and these are the two extremes, and most people fall in between some place-- who love it. I took to the United States like duck takes to water. I loved it from the day-- that's not true. I loved it long before I arrived. And-- and my love affair with America has grown since 1947. Now, every morning-- we have a small apartment very near the capital, and my wife and I and our little dog walk to work. And when I look up, the dome and the flag, I choke up every morning. To have a penniless Jewish immigrant from Budapest, a survivor of the Holocaust, be given the privilege of serving in Congress and serving his area and the country is a reward nobody deserves. I've always been public service-oriented. When I got my bachelor's and master's degree at the University of Washington, then I went down to Berkeley and got my PhD in economics at Berkeley. Even before I got it, I started teaching. I already taught at the University of Washington. I actually taught a few months after I arrived. I got myself a teaching position in-- at the University of Washington. The only thing I was qualified to teach was Hungarian, and I persuaded the president of the university that the greatness of a university is measured by the variety of languages it teaches, and the only university that was teaching Hungarian at that time was the University of Indiana in Bloomington, and nobody else taught it. And I-- I persuaded the president that it will be a feather in the cap of the University of Washington if they also will teach Hungarian. And he was a very nice fellow. His name was Raymond Allen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said, well, we will hire you as our young instructor of Hungarian language and literature. Then my problem came to find students because who would want in-- in-- in Seattle, which is a Scandinavian area? So we succeeded in-- in recruiting a number of students. And I think I was the youngest faculty member in the history of that university. Started to teach, I believe, in January of '48.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I basically taught from January of '48 until the fall of '80, when I was elected to Congress. I did a few other things in between, but basically I-- I was in university teaching for 32 years. For 10 years, first I established and I directed the study abroad program for the state university and college system in California. And I've been privileged to serve in Congress now for 16 years. I'm about to begin my 17th year.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your major motivator to go into politics?

speaker: interviewee
text: I view politics as public service. I-- I realize that people are cynical about government and politics. I am not. I-- I-- I believe with Winston Churchill that democracy is the worst form of government except all others that have been tried. And if decent and honest people don't get into public life, somebody else will get into public life, so I am passionately committed to public life. One of my great joys was that our-- our younger daughter's husband, who is a professional architect, was convinced by me to do this and was elected to the Congress of the United States. And one of my great joys was working with him in the Congress as a colleague. I very much hope at least some of my grandchildren will go into public service. I have 17.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] approached public service, how old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's a difficult one because I first learned about American government, apart from the university courses, from my friend in Alaska who was a-- who was and is a very political animal. So I had an excellent sort of private tutor about American government. My wife and I got married in 1950, and I remember that I was very active as a volunteer in a Congressional race in 1950 for the Democratic candidate whose name was Harold Taggart, and who lost the race. And 30 years later, I won that seat. Then in 1952, I organized what we called at that time the Stevenson-Sparkman Democratic Club in my town. I was its first president. I worked for Stevenson. I worked very hard with Stevenson. In-- in '56 I worked very hard with Stevenson on his second campaign. I worked very hard with Senator Alan Cranston in California, with Governor Pat Brown.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I have been involved with politics certainly since the early '50s. And then when our older daughter started elementary school, the path to her school I deemed very unsafe because there were cars going very near it. And I went down to the school board, and I proposed that they move this path where these adorable little six-year-old boys and girls were walking about 20 feet inside so they will be far from the cars. And I was treated very badly by the school board, and they don't-- they told me to get lost. So I ran for the school board, and I won. And I served eight years on that school board and poured my heart into that. I certainly don't claim credit, but by the time I finished my service as president of the school board, that school district had the highest scores of its children in the whole state of California. We paid the highest salaries to teachers. We had the smallest class size. We had a marvelous program for gifted children with a marvelous program for mentally and physically retarded children.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in some ways, those eight years were unbelievably satisfying. And I really learned firsthand that nobody will come to you and ask you to get into public service. You throw your hat in the ring. And-- and that's what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: My-- my Hungarian friends, when I was first elected to Congress, asked me, quite a few of them, how did the people in your district-- and I have, you know, about 600,000 people in the district-- select you, with-- an immigrant, a Jew with an accent? I said, nobody selected me. I selected myself. I stepped forward, I organized my campaign, I raised the money, I ran my campaign, and I was elected. And that's one of the reasons why I love this country. I mean, nowhere else can this happen. We're gonna stop again for a minute.
